NATIONAL ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT & LAW CENTER
I95O ADDISON STREET . BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704

Practices Cornmission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramerrto, cA
Attn: Jonathan
Re3 A-88-4 2 2
:rlou{-uou/
Rothman
Dear Mr. Rothman:
The City of Berkeley requested an opinion regarding the inclusion
of the South Berkeley Cornmunity Housing Development Corporation
within the definition 
of a local government agency in 1988
resulting in the opinion referenced above, finding the organization
to be such an agency.
At this time I would request a review of whether or not the
organization remains a local governrnent agency in light of changes
in circumstances and what circumstances, if any, might change its
status in the future. 
The original decision relied on the four
part test in In re Sieqel (1977) 3FPPC Ops. 62. The following are
the changes I am aware of and are organized by the structure of the
original opinion including the criteria of In re Sieqel.
FACTS
The originai opiniorr j.rrdicaies +-ha+- :r city appcinted ccrunlttee
recommended the formation of a new corporation and that the
cornrnittee be the board of directors of that corporation. In the
intervening three years there has been turnover on the board of
directors. currently only 5 of the origina] 11 board menbers are
still 
in office. 
The City has had no role in appoj.nting the
replacernent board members. This function is carried out by the
renaining directors. 
The board is currentl-y contenplating one of
its original purposes, which was to bring residents of the property
onto the board as nembers, and to turnover control of the board to
the residents within a fixed period of time, makj.ng the property a
limited eguity cooperative as training and experience pernit.


Jonathan Rothman
ANALY S I S
-2-
September 1l- , l-991
Loan, City of Berkeley
Loan, City of Berkel-ey
crant, City of Berkel,ey
Total Funding, City of Berkeley
Private Financing
Tax credit Syndication Proceeds
Loan, State of CaI i fornia
Total Funding, Other Sources
Total Development Cost
Whether the impetus for formation of the corporation oriqinated
with a government aqency.
No new facts, other than the change in board membership and
appointnent authority, have surfaced sj-nce the original opinion.
whether the corporation is substantiallv funded by, or its primarv
source of funds is, a qovernment aqencv.
Initially 
the housing development corporation received funding in
the forn of a Loan for its predevelopment work from the City of
BerkeLey. Tirat i=undinE was the soie source of suppcrr: at +-he tj-ne
of the original opinion. Since that tj-rne it has received funding
from numerous other sources. The following is a current listing of
funding sources 3
$804, r-54
500, 000
425 , OOO
L,729,]-54
1-,3oo,ooo
950,0o0
1f 28O, 000
3,530, ooo
5 ,259 , 154
In addition the City is providing the Land under a long term Lease
for only $1 per year. The financing package appraised the value of
the leasehold interest at some$rhere in the area of $2OO,oOO.
This would indicate that the City is stilf 
a funder of the housing
development corporation but no longer the prirnary one. It is
sonev;hat unclear to ne rvhat i'u rneans to be rr substantially funded
by't the City.
Whether one of the principal purposes for the formation of the
corporation is to provide services or undertake obliqations which
pubLic aqencies are l-eqaltv and traditionally 
authorized to
perform.
The original 
opinion indicates that the housing development
corporation neets this test because affordable housing is involved.
The opinion cites covernment Code Section 37364 giving cities the
authority to utilize real property to provide affordable housing to
farnilies of Lov/ or rnoderate income. No change has occurred here.
It shoul-d be noted, however, that only a portion of the units fron
the beginning $/ere designed to be affordable. It has always been
intended to be a mixed income project with rnore than half of the


Jonathan Rothman
-3-
September 11, 199 L
units set at market rate. 
Does that make a difference?
Whether the conooration is treated as a public entitv bv other
statutory provisions.
The original 
opinj,on indicated that the housinq developrnent
corporation failed to meet this test but that not all tests had to
be met for an entity to qualify as a l-oca1 governrnent agency. I am
aware of no new circumstances that would bring the housing
developnent corporation within thi-s further test.
Conc Ius ions
The original opinion makes clear that not ali criteria have 'to be
met. clearly the fourth is not necessary. The opinion is silent
on ho!, many and in what fashion the other criteria have to be met
for there to be a finding of inclusion as a local- governnent
agency.
The impetus test historically 
woul,d indicate sone forn of control
over the organization. 
If the inpetus test means only that
historically there was control over the origin of the eorporation
rather than continuing control , it should not be enough alone to
bring the organization within the definition of a loca1 government
agency. If the organj-zation is no longer subject to any control by
locaI government and starts doing things total-1y different than
what was originally intended, it sounds difficult 
to believe that
it would still 
be a locat government agency.
Here the facts would indicate that the City no Longer controLs who
serves on the board and that a majority of the cityts appointments
are no l-onger serving. 
Is this test net forever if it is net
initially, 
or does the real force of the test cease once the City
no longer exercises control ?
The control becones most tenuous when the loca1 residents of the
project, none of whon $re=e appointed bi, the Cir-)', becone a majority
of the board. How does the board function then? This control by
the residents was the intent both of the originaL board and of the
city. 
Each of the resident board menbers wilt fa11 under very
strict interpretation rules as to any conflict of interest because
they rent within the project. 
It wiII be impossible to pass many
necessary resolutions. 
what solutions to this problem can your
office suggest?
The rrsubstantially funded by't test nay no longer be met. The City
i:.lgt 
the prirnary funder of the orga-nizatiori. Although [ire clty
sti11 provides substantial funds, the test is whetier it 
i3
rrsubstantially funded by'. the city, which may connote sonething
closer to the notion of being the piimary funde-r. rf thi.s tist now
or in the future is no rongdr met, is tirat sufficient to take the
organization out from under r.ocal governrnent agency status, oa ua"


Jonathan Rothman
-4-
September l,L, 1991
the first 
and third criteria sufficient by themselves to make the
organization a 1ocal government aqency?
The third criterion r,rould seem to have fittle 
weight by itself. 
If
it is sufficient that the City have authority to carry out the
activities of the nonprofit, as the original opinion rrlould seem to
say, this crj.terion would seem just a quaLifier of the first 
two.
The City is also authorized and traditionally has conveyed property
to historical associations and low incorne individuals. 
No one
$/ould argue, I assume, that nonprofits that convey property to
these types of indivj.duals are inherently 1ocaI goverrunent agencies
wit-hout meeting the other tests. The City is authorized to operate
a motor pool . 
If 
it 
contracts with a private corporation
established by a local busirressman in part to provide services to
the City, does that nake the private corporation a local government
agency? It is the conjunction of the third criterion with the
first two would seern to make it important. The first two appear to
be, and logically should be, the most important for a finding that
the nonprofit is a Iocal government agency. If either or both of
the first 
two criteria 
have changed, the fact that housing is
involved should not be enough to perpetuate the locaL government
agency status.
The question then becomes !,/hether the nonprofit still 
meets the
second test and the real intent of the first test, and whether the
first 
test, without the second test, could alr^rays bring this
nonprofit within the definition of a IocaL government agency. The
nonprofit would appreciate an indication of whether the impetus
test wilI alv,rays tar it with the status of being a local" government
agency, because it currentl"y faces the prospect of not being able
to function as more and more board mernbers, as residents of the
property, will have conflicts of interest under the FppC test but
not under the Corporations code.
I appreciate your consideration of this request for an opinion.
Attorney at Law

sections.question: Whether the South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation remains a local government agency in light of changes in circumstances, including: (1) board membership turnover with City no longer appointing replacements; (2) diversified funding sources with City no longer the primary funder; (3) planned transition of board control to residents; and (4) potential conflicts of interest issues under FPPC rules as resident board members increase. Specifically, whether the 'impetus test' requires continuing control or only historical origin; whether the 'substantially funded by' test is still met; whether the third criterion has sufficient weight alone; and what solutions exist for conflicts of interest as residents become board majority.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation, originally designated as a local government agency under the In re Sieqel test, retains that status given changes in board appointment authority, funding diversification, and planned resident control transition.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney, not an FPPC response. The document contains detailed FACTS and ANALYSIS sections but no CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER from FPPC, as this is the initial request. The question_synthetic field provides comprehensive coverage of the complex multi-part inquiry. OCR quality is generally good with minor formatting irregularities.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether the South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation remains a local government agency in light of changes in circumstances, including: (1) board membership turnover with City no longer appointing replacements; (2) diversified funding sources with City no longer the primary funder; (3) planned transition of board control to residents; and (4) potential conflicts of interest issues under FPPC rules as resident board members increase. Specifically, whether the 'impetus test' requires continuing control or only historical origin; whether the 'substantially funded by' test is still met; whether the third criterion has sufficient weight alone; and what solutions exist for conflicts of interest as residents become board majority.

embedding.summary: An attorney representing South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation requests FPPC review of whether the organization continues to qualify as a local government agency, given significant changes since the original 1988 opinion: board turnover with no City appointments of replacements, diversified funding sources reducing City's role to non-primary funder, and planned transition of board control to residents. The attorney analyzes each prong of the In re Sieqel test and raises questions about the test's application to changing circumstances and potential conflicts of interest issues.
